questions.question: Explain the Cohen-Sutherland line clipping algorithm with its outcode bits.
keyPoints: Explain 4-bit outcode representation (top, bottom, right, left)
Describe trivial accept (both endpoints 0000) condition
Describe trivial reject (AND of outcodes ≠ 0) condition
Mention iterative endpoint clipping process

questions.question: Derive the window-to-viewport transformation equations.
keyPoints: Show scaling factors: sx = (vxmax-vxmin)/(wxmax-wxmin)
Show sy = (vymax-vymin)/(wymax-wymin)
Show translation components for x and y
Explain how combined transformation maps window points to viewport

questions.question: Compare orthographic and perspective projections with respect to projection lines and depth perception.
keyPoints: Orthographic: parallel projection lines, no depth distortion
Perspective: converging lines at center of projection
Perspective creates foreshortening effect
Orthographic used in engineering, perspective in realistic rendering

questions.question: Describe the UVN camera model for defining the viewing coordinate system.
keyPoints: Camera position (COP) as origin
N vector = direction camera looks (negative Z of camera)
V vector = upward direction in camera space
U vector = rightward direction (V × N)

questions.question: Explain the importance of near and far clipping planes in 3D viewing.
keyPoints: Near plane prevents division by zero and culls geometry behind camera
Far plane limits view distance and improves depth buffer precision
Together they define view volume depth
Help in precision management of z-buffer

questions.question: What is the viewing pipeline? List all transformations in sequence.
keyPoints: Modeling transformation (world coordinates)
Viewing transformation (to camera coordinates)
Projection transformation (to canonical view volume)
Clipping (against view volume)
Window-to-viewport transformation (to screen coordinates)
Device transformation (to actual screen pixels)

questions.question: Explain Liang-Barsky line clipping algorithm and its advantages over Cohen-Sutherland.
keyPoints: Parametric form of line clipping
Computes intersection parameters directly
More efficient as it computes exact clipping values
Handles clipping against all four boundaries in single pass

questions.question: Explain the complete 3D viewing pipeline with a neat diagram. Describe the purpose of each stage.
keyPoints: Diagram showing: World coords → Modeling → Viewing → Projection → Clipping → Viewport → Device
Modeling: positions objects in world
Viewing: transforms to camera coordinates using UVN
Projection: projects to 2D view plane (orthographic or perspective)
Clipping: removes objects outside view frustum
Viewport: maps NDC to screen coordinates
Device: actual pixel rendering

questions.question: Derive the perspective projection matrix and explain how it transforms 3D points to 2D on the view plane.
keyPoints: Define center of projection and view plane
Show projection equations: xp = x*d/z, yp = y*d/z
Derive homogeneous coordinate transformation matrix
Explain how division by w creates perspective effect
Mention field of view and aspect ratio considerations

questions.question: Explain the Sutherland-Hodgman polygon clipping algorithm. How does it clip polygons against each clipping window edge?
keyPoints: Clips polygon against each edge of window sequentially
For each edge: consider each vertex pair
If both inside: output second vertex
If first inside, second outside: output intersection
If first outside, second inside: output intersection and second vertex
Handles concave polygons with limitations

questions.question: What is the viewing frustum? Explain how perspective and orthographic view volumes differ and their effect on the rendered image.
keyPoints: View frustum = 3D region visible to camera
Perspective: truncated pyramid (near plane smaller than far plane)
Orthographic: parallelepiped (parallel sides)
Perspective creates depth with foreshortening
Orthographic maintains parallel lines, used for measurements
Near/far planes affect precision and visibility range
